text.title: OKR 6-Step Process
text.summary: Sequential steps from strategy alignment through weekly tracking cadence.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Align to strategy. Inputs: company mission/vision, annual goals, previous quarter's learnings and scores, current market conditions. The anchoring question: "What must we accomplish this quarter to move toward our vision?"
rule.rationale: OKRs disconnected from strategy are vanity. The "why this matters" sentence per Objective is more important than the Objective wording itself. Lose the why and OKRs become prose.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Draft Objectives by brainstorming. Ask: "What is most important right now?", "If we could only achieve one thing, what would it be?", "What would make this quarter a success?" Limit to 3-5 Objectives maximum.
rule.rationale: Brainstorming before constraining prevents anchoring to last quarter's OKRs. The single-thing question forces prioritization before KRs are written.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Define Key Results for each Objective. For each KR: how will we know we achieved the Objective? What metric matters? What is the specific target? What is today's baseline? Can it be measured without building new instrumentation?
rule.rationale: The instrumentation question prevents phantom KRs — KRs that sound measurable but require 6 weeks of data pipeline work before they can be tracked at all.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Validate. Check: Objectives are qualitative, KRs are measurable outcomes (not tasks), 70% confidence per KR, connected to higher-level goals, total count is focused.
rule.rationale: Validation before commitment catches KR-as-task and missing baselines when they are cheapest to fix — before the quarter starts.

rule.statement: Step 5 — Communicate and commit. Share with stakeholders, get explicit buy-in, make visible on a shared dashboard or wiki. Add a kill condition per Objective: "We drop this Objective if [leading indicator] does not move by week 6."
rule.rationale: Kill conditions prevent OKR theatre — beautiful OKRs that no one acts on. A kill condition forces a mid-quarter honest assessment rather than waiting for the retrospective.

rule.statement: Step 6 — Track weekly with confidence (High/Med/Low), not progress percent. Confidence is forward-looking; percent is backward-looking. Score KRs at end of quarter on 0.0-1.0 scale. Append to the record; do not replace it.
rule.rationale: Confidence tracking surfaces risk earlier. A KR at 80% progress with Low confidence is a bigger problem than a KR at 30% progress with High confidence. Weekly appends preserve the trend history that the retrospective depends on.

text.reference: Quarterly OKR document with baseline/target/current/score table
Python linter to catch task-shaped and unbaselined KRs before commitment
